title: The connection between eczema flares and emotional activation
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
Your skin is not just skin

I remember the first time I noticed it. I was sitting in my car after a phone call that left me shaking. My mother's voice, sharp and familiar, had found its way under my skin. Literally. Within hours, my arms were covered in red, angry patches. The itching was unbearable. I wanted to claw my way out of my own body.

Here's the thing. I thought it was just me. I thought I was broken in some unique way, that my skin had some mysterious allergy to stress. But the science tells a different story. And it's a story that might change how you see your own flares.

Your skin is your largest organ. It's also your most exposed. It's the boundary between you and the world. And when that boundary is compromised, when you're living with chronic eczema, it's not just a skin problem. It's a nervous system problem. It's a trauma problem. It's a whole-body problem.

Stay with me here. Because I know how tempting it is to reach for another tube of cortisone, to blame the weather, to blame your diet, to blame yourself. I've been there. I've spent thousands on creams and elimination diets and special soaps. And some of that helped. But none of it touched the root.

The root lives deeper. In your brainstem. In your vagus nerve. In the places where your body stores the things you never learned how to say.

The science of skin and emotion

Bessel van der Kolk, the psychiatrist who wrote "The Body Keeps the Score," spent decades studying how trauma lives in the body. He found that people who experienced early adversity had higher rates of autoimmune conditions, including skin disorders. His work showed that the body doesn't forget. Even when the mind tries to move on, the body holds the memory.

And here's what that means for your eczema. Your skin is packed with nerve endings. It's wired directly into your limbic system, the part of your brain that processes threat and safety. When your nervous system detects danger - even emotional danger, even the memory of an old wound - it sends signals to your skin. Inflammation increases. Histamine releases. The barrier function weakens.

You're not imagining it. Your flares are real. And they're connected to what you feel.

Think about it. When was your last bad flare? What was happening in your life? Were you under pressure at work? Fighting with a partner? Feeling unseen or unheard? I'm not saying every flare is caused by emotion. There are real triggers - allergens, irritants, weather changes. But the emotional piece is real too. And ignoring it means you're fighting only half the battle.

Gabor Mate, the physician and author, has written extensively about this connection. He talks about how the immune system and the emotional brain are not separate systems. They're the same system. They evolved together. They speak the same language. And when you suppress emotion, when you push down anger or grief or fear, your body finds another way to express it. Sometimes that expression comes through the skin.

I know. That's a hard thing to sit with. It was hard for me too. Because it means that healing isn't just about finding the right cream. It's about feeling the things you've been avoiding. It's about letting your body speak.

A book that connects the dots between stress and illness is When the Body Says No (paid link). Mate's work in that book helped me understand that my skin wasn't betraying me. It was trying to protect me. It was saying the things I couldn't say.

The vagus nerve and your skin barrier

Here's where it gets really interesting. Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed polyvagal theory, discovered that the vagus nerve plays a direct role in inflammation. The vagus nerve is the main highway between your brain and your body. It runs from your brainstem down to your gut, touching your heart, your lungs, your digestive organs along the way. And it has a branch that connects to your skin.

When your vagus nerve is working well, it sends signals of safety to your body. Your heart rate slows. Your digestion works. Your immune system calms down. But when your vagus nerve is compromised - by chronic stress, by trauma, by the constant activation of your fight-or-flight response - it stops sending those safety signals. Your body stays on high alert. Inflammation becomes chronic. And your skin becomes a battleground.

Wild, right? Your skin condition might be a vagus nerve condition. It might be a signal that your nervous system is stuck in survival mode.

I've seen this in my own body. When I'm regulated, when I feel safe and grounded, my skin calms down. The redness fades. The itching stops. But when I'm triggered, when something reminds me of an old wound or a current threat, the flares come back. It's like my skin is a weather vane for my nervous system.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice (paid link). Peter Levine's work on somatic experiencing showed me that the body has its own wisdom. It knows how to release stored trauma. It just needs the right conditions.

A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link). Deb Dana's work in that book offers concrete exercises for shifting your nervous system state. And when your nervous system shifts, your skin often follows.

What actually helps

I'm not going to tell you to stop using your creams. I'm not going to tell you that it's all in your head. That would be dismissive and wrong. Your eczema is real. The inflammation is real. The itch is real. And you deserve relief.

But I am going to suggest that you add another layer to your approach. A layer that addresses the nervous system.

Start by noticing. Notice what happens in your body when you think about a stressful situation. Does your skin prickle? Does your chest tighten? Do you feel heat rising? Just notice. Don't try to change it. Don't judge it. Let your body have its experience.

Then try something simple. Put your hand on the area that's flaring. Not to scratch, just to hold. Breathe into that hand. Let your breath be slow and easy. This is a vagus nerve exercise. It sends signals of safety to your brain. And your brain sends those signals back to your skin.

I know it sounds too simple. I thought so too. But I've seen it work. Not overnight. Not perfectly. But over time, the flares became less frequent. Less intense. And when they did come, I understood them differently. They weren't enemies to be defeated. They were messages to be heard.

A tool that many find helpful for releasing stored body tension is TriggerPoint GRID Foam Roller (paid link). Using it on your back and shoulders can help release the physical tension that often accompanies emotional stress. And when your body releases tension, your nervous system can shift.

Here's the uncomfortable truth. Healing your skin might mean healing your history. It might mean feeling the grief you've been avoiding. It might mean setting a boundary with someone who drains you. It might mean letting yourself be angry when you've spent a lifetime being nice.

I'm not saying this to overwhelm you. I'm saying it because I wish someone had said it to me. I spent years treating my skin as an isolated problem. I tried everything external. And some of it helped, temporarily. But the real shift happened when I started listening to what my body was trying to tell me.

Your body is not your enemy. Your eczema is not a punishment. It's a signal. It's a voice. And when you learn to hear it, something shifts. Not just in your skin. In your whole life.

Sit with that.

Healing Journey Resources

  • In an Unspoken Voice (paid link) - Peter Levine's exploration of how the body holds and releases trauma
  • When the Body Says No (paid link) - Gabor Mate's work on the connection between stress and illness
  • The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link) - Deb Dana's practical guide to working with the nervous system
  • TriggerPoint GRID Foam Roller (paid link) - A tool for releasing physical tension stored in the body
